title: Schoolhouse Desk Hutch
summary: An open-shelf hutch that sits on top of a desk, with a corkboard or panel back for pinning notes above the work surface.
description:
This is a simple open box with dividers, but it carries real weight up high on top of a desk — books, bins, whatever lands on those shelves — so the desk-mounting brackets and the wall attachment aren't optional extras, they're what keeps a top-heavy hutch from tipping forward onto whoever's sitting at the desk. Do both.

The back panel is a good spot to upgrade the plan: corkboard or wainscoting paneling both work as well as plywood and turn the whole back into either a pinboard or a bit of visual texture, for about the same material cost. Fill every screw head with wood filler before you sand and finish — on an open piece like this, every surface is visible from across the room.

materials.name: Plywood, corkboard, or wainscoting panel, 1/4"
materials.note: 46-1/2" x 31" back panel — corkboard turns it into a pinboard for about the same cost.

materials.name: Desk-mounting brackets
materials.note: Secures the hutch to the desk; also anchor to the wall behind to prevent forward tipping.

cutList.note: Cut to fit — sides may vary slightly.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the two 8 ft 1x10s cut the top at 46-1/2", two sides at 30-1/4", the bottom shelf at 45" and two dividers at 8-1/2", all at the board's full 9-1/4" width. From the 10 ft 1x2 cut the bottom trim at 45", the top front trim at 48" and two side trims at 9-1/4"; from the 4 ft 1x4 cut the front trim rail at 45". Cut the back panel 46-1/2" x 31" from whichever backing you chose — plywood, corkboard or wainscoting all work. Cut the two 30-1/4" sides clamped together as a pair so they match exactly; on a case this wide, a sixteenth of an inch between the sides twists the whole box.

steps.title: Attach the sides to the top
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes across the top end of both 30-1/4" sides, on the inside faces. Lay the 46-1/2" top face down and bring a side up under each end with its outside face flush with the end of the top, then drive 1-1/4" pocket hole screws with glue in the joint. Check each corner with the speed square as you fasten — the top does not overhang, so the case's inside width is set here, and the 45" bottom shelf will only drop in if both corners are true.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf to the sides
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in both ends of the 45" bottom shelf and fit it between the sides. Its height is what sets the cubbies: the dividers are 8-1/2", so hold the shelf 8-1/2" below the underside of the top, which leaves the tall open bay beneath it for the desk surface. Glue and screw it home, then compare the case's diagonals and rack it true before anything else goes on.

steps.title: Add the dividers
steps.body: Stand the two 8-1/2" dividers between the top and the bottom shelf to break the upper section into three cubbies. Three bays across the 45" span with two 3/4" dividers works out to 14-1/2" each — mark both divider positions from the same end of the shelf so a measuring error doesn't compound across the run. Drill pocket holes in both ends of each divider, pointing into whichever bay you'd rather not look into, and screw through into the top and the shelf with glue.

steps.title: Add back supports
steps.body: Run 1x2 strips along the inside back edges of the sides and the bottom shelf so the back panel fastens into something rather than into 3/4" panel edges. Hold each strip in from the back by the thickness of your backing — 1/4" for plywood or corkboard — so the panel finishes flush with the back of the case. Measure these off the assembled case: the cut list has no row for them and the 10 ft 1x2 is almost entirely used up by the trim, so plan on extra 1x2 for this step.

steps.title: Attach the front trim
steps.body: Face the front: the 45" 1x4 front trim rail across the bottom shelf's front edge, the 45" 1x2 bottom trim, the two 9-1/4" 1x2 side trims down the outside faces of the sides, then the 48" 1x2 across the top. Fit the side trims before the top piece — 48" is the case's 46-1/2" plus a 3/4" side trim at each end, so with them on, the top piece lands flush at both ends with nothing to trim. Glue and pin everything with 1" and 1-1/4" finish nails, setting the heads below the surface for filling later.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Lay the case face down and set the 46-1/2" x 31" back panel on it. Line it up with one side and the top and tack those edges, then push the case until it meets the panel's other two edges before nailing the rest — the panel is cut to the case's true size (30-1/4" sides plus the 3/4" top is exactly 31"), so if it doesn't fit, the case is racked rather than the panel mis-cut. Glue and nail into the back support strips with a nail length suited to your backing: 1" nails go through 1/4" corkboard into the strip without coming through the front.

steps.title: Mount to the desk and wall, then finish
steps.body: Do both attachments, not one. Bolt the hutch to the desk with the mounting brackets, and drive at least two screws through the back into wall studs behind it. Loaded with books this piece carries real weight up high, and the wall anchor is the only thing that stops it tipping forward onto whoever is sitting at the desk. Then fill every nail and screw hole with wood filler, sand the whole piece flush — on an open hutch every surface is visible from across the room — and finish as desired.

images.alt: Schoolhouse-style desk hutch with open cubby shelves and a corkboard back panel
